title: The Minor Poems of Robert Henryson (twelve-poem selection)
summary: The repository artifact is a copy or version of University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive electronic item U-243-A, made in 1993 from G. Gregory Smith's 1908 Scottish Text Society edition. The electronic item is an edited selection of twelve separately headed shorter poems, not one poem and not every work that Smith or a modern complete edition groups among Henryson's shorter poems. Smith's selected texts derive from ten passages in the Bannatyne manuscript, the sole Gray-manuscript copy of The Annunciation, and the 1508 Chepman and Myllar print of The Prais of Aige; his apparatus and parallel texts also use Bannatyne alternatives, the Maitland Folio, and the Makculloch manuscript. Authorship is not uniform: several selected witnesses name Henryson, some selected versions are anonymous but have attributed collateral versions, The Prayer for the Pest has a later-hand Henryson ascription, and the selected Bannatyne text of The Thre Deid Pollis names Patrick Johnstoun while the Maitland version names Henryson.
review_notes: The cataloging boundary is the twelve DIV1 units actually present in the pinned XML. Smith also prints collateral versions and other shorter poems, including The Want of Wise Men, that are not in HenMino.

Bannatyne draft and main-text passages are parts of one surviving codex, NLS Adv.MS.1.1.6; the codex's current two-volume binding and two digital manifests are facsimile divisions, not separate textual witnesses.

Exact folio, historical page, canvas, and tested image-API links are recorded for every selected manuscript passage. Page images can include text before or after the selected poem at shared boundaries.

For The Thre Deid Pollis, direct inspection places the selected text at current fols. 57v-59r, historical pages 174-177, canvases 192-195. CELM's combined folio/page locator is internally inconsistent because page 178 is the following work.
Gray-manuscript foliation is stated in both current NLS form (fols. 71r-72v) and the historical form used by Smith and CELM (fols. 70r-71v).

Access, the public-domain status of underlying historical texts, licenses or rights statements for modern files, and provider reproduction terms are separate assertions. Public viewing does not by itself establish permission to redistribute images.
The XML's c. 1480 creation date and blanket Henryson heading are legacy collection-level assertions; they are not allowed to erase poem-level date and authorship uncertainty.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for HenMino
entities.description: The pinned XML artifact consumed by this repository and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: File size is 47,552 bytes at the reviewed commit.

entities.label: University of Michigan / Oxford Text Archive electronic item HenMino, U-243-A
entities.description: A 1993 electronic edition of twelve selected minor poems. Its header identifies the broader OTA accession U*-243-A, Smith's three-volume Poems of Robert Henryson as source, Scottish Text Society number 58 as the source volume, and the omission of printed front matter, back matter, and editorial notes.
notes: The shared OTA identifier does not establish that every U-243-A item is one encoding entity; HenMino is modeled as the identified U-M item.
No current standalone OTA accession record giving a more granular production history for this 1993 item was verified.

entities.label: G. Gregory Smith, The Poems of Robert Henryson, volume III (1908)
entities.description: Scottish Text Society number 58, volume III overall and text volume II. Smith prints separately labeled witness versions, identifies the selected sources in a prefatory table, and supplies the page sequence reproduced imperfectly by HenMino's PB milestones.

bibliographic.citation: The Poems of Robert Henryson, ed. G. Gregory Smith, vol. III, Text vol. II, Scottish Text Society 58 (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood and Sons, 1908).
bibliographic.title: The Poems of Robert Henryson

notes: Smith thanks George Stevenson for transcribing and collating texts; Stevenson is therefore an edition-layer transcriber and collator, not a creator of the medieval works.

The source table counts Sum Practysis of Medecyne as 90 lines, but the printed text and XML contain a final five-line tail numbered through 91; the XML's numbered line 91 is not an extra subscription.
Smith's source table and printed headings distinguish selected versions from collateral variants.

entities.label: G. Gregory Smith, The Poems of Robert Henryson, volume I (1914)
entities.description: The introduction, appendix, notes, glossary, and indexes volume of Smith's edition. It supplies source and authorship discussion omitted from the HenMino electronic item.

bibliographic.citation: The Poems of Robert Henryson, ed. G. Gregory Smith, vol. I, Introduction, Appendix, Notes, Index of Words and Glossary, General Index (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood and Sons, 1914).
bibliographic.title: The Poems of Robert Henryson

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Smith volume III (1908)
entities.description: A complete 232-page public PDF with OCR, scan metadata, and item metadata cached for source verification.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF, OCR text, and scan-data XML

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Smith volume I (1914)
entities.description: A complete 360-page public PDF with OCR, scan metadata, and item metadata cached for critical-note verification.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF, OCR text, and scan-data XML

entities.label: Bannatyne manuscript, NLS Adv.MS.1.1.6
entities.description: George Bannatyne's 1568 paper anthology. The current codex has a sixty-page draft section followed by the main manuscript and was rebound into two physical volumes around 1823. HenMino's selected Smith texts use ten passages across the draft, main volume I, and main volume II.

holding.notes: The present two-volume arrangement is a nineteenth-century physical intervention; draft and main sections are textual strata within this single codex.

physical_description.extent: 375 folios plus sixty draft pages in the catalog description; present volume I has 192 leaves and volume II 205 leaves
physical_description.format: Composite poetry anthology now bound in two volumes
notes: The main manuscript has current foliation 1-370 and later pagination 1-796; that pagination is also used to locate the draft pages.

entities.label: NLS digital facsimile of Bannatyne manuscript volume I
entities.description: The complete 424-canvas IIIF object for current physical volume I, covering the full draft and the first part of the main manuscript.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and 424 JPEG derivatives at 1600-pixel width

entities.label: NLS digital facsimile of Bannatyne manuscript volume II
entities.description: The complete 454-canvas IIIF object for current physical volume II, the latter part of the main manuscript.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and 454 JPEG derivatives at 1600-pixel width

entities.label: Gray manuscript, NLS Adv.MS.34.7.3
entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century Scottish composite commonplace book associated with James Gray and written in several hands. It is the sole known witness for The Annunciation and supplies Smith's selected text.

holding.notes: The Annunciation is at current fols. 71r-72v; Smith and CELM use historical fols. 70r-71v.

notes: Several hands contribute to the manuscript; association with James Gray is a compilation/provenance statement, not automatic authorship of its contents.

entities.label: NLS digital facsimile of the Gray manuscript
entities.description: The complete 242-canvas IIIF digital object. The manifest assigns RightsStatements.org In Copyright - Rights-holder(s) Unlocatable or Unidentifiable, not a public-domain mark.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and 242 JPEG derivatives at 1600-pixel width

entities.label: Chepman and Myllar's 1508 print containing The Prais of Aige
entities.description: The anonymous thirty-two-line print version selected by Smith, published in Edinburgh in the unique NLS collection of Chepman and Myllar prints. The schema has no dedicated historical-print-witness type, so this source print is represented as a scholarly-edition entity with that limitation made explicit.

bibliographic.citation: The Prais of Aige, in the Chepman and Myllar prints (Edinburgh: Walter Chepman and Androw Myllar, 1508), tract 7.
bibliographic.title: The Prais of Aige

holding.notes: The current precise shelfmark was not established from the reviewed public project pages; the unique surviving collection was rebound and its leaves separately mounted in the twentieth century.

entities.label: Complete 220-image public derivative of the Chepman and Myllar prints
entities.description: A complete 220-image DjVu derivative publicly served by Wikimedia Commons. Complete refers to the published facsimile image sequence, not to physical boards or flyleaves absent from that sequence.

entities.label: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553
entities.description: A c. 1570-1585 Scots poetry anthology associated with Sir Richard Maitland. Smith prints or collates collateral versions of The Abbey Walk, Aganis Haisty Credence of Titlaris, The Ressoning betuix Aige and Zowth, and The Thre Deid Pollis; none is HenMino's selected base version.

holding.notes: The complete manuscript is not in the current online digital selection; research access is by advance contact, and the Pepys Library is closed to general visitors during refurbishment until 2027.

notes: Several hands copied the collection.

entities.label: Makculloch manuscript, University of Edinburgh MS 205
entities.description: A composite manuscript whose 1477 Leuven logic notes belong to an earlier host layer; early-sixteenth-century Scots verse was added later on flyleaves and blanks. Smith prints or collates collateral versions of The Prais of Aige at fol. 87r and The Ressoning betuix Aige and Zowth at fol. 181v.

holding.notes: Open to consultation in the Centre for Research Collections; the public record exposes only selected images, not a complete digital facsimile.

entities.label: Untraced Abbey Walk copy reported by David Riddell in 1636
entities.description: CELM records an additional copy of The Abbey Walk reported in David Riddell's 1636 list but not presently traced. It is evidence of a reported witness, not an extant text available for collation.
notes: Its former holding, text, and relationship to the extant Bannatyne and Maitland versions remain unknown.

entities.label: CELM catalogue and introduction for Robert Henryson
entities.description: A current scholarly witness census with manuscript locations and source descriptions, used to distinguish selected and collateral versions and to identify untraced evidence.

entities.label: METS current complete edition of Robert Henryson
entities.description: A current Middle English Texts Series edition consulted for language, scope, witnesses, interpretation, and authorship cautions. It is not the source of the 1993 encoding and does not necessarily select the same version for every poem.
entities.reproduction_format: 153-page downloadable PDF and web reading edition

editorial_practices.source_feature: Multiple manuscript and print versions of individual shorter poems
editorial_practices.representation: One labeled version is selected for each HenMino poem while other versions are printed separately or collated
editorial_practices.description: Smith's prefatory source table fixes the version represented by each HenMino text: ten Bannatyne passages, one Gray-manuscript passage, and one Chepman and Myllar print passage.
editorial_practices.scope: The twelve texts encoded in HenMino

editorial_practices.source_feature: Variant copies in Bannatyne, Maitland, and Makculloch manuscripts
editorial_practices.representation: Parallel versions, comparison, and editorial apparatus
editorial_practices.description: Smith and credited transcriber/collator George Stevenson use collateral versions without making every collateral copy an ancestor of the selected electronic text.
editorial_practices.scope: Prais of Aige, Aige and Zowth, Abbey Walk, Deth and Man, Aganis, Thre Deid Pollis, and Prayer for the Pest

editorial_practices.source_feature: Smith's full volume, including front matter, back matter, notes, collateral versions, and other shorter poems
editorial_practices.description: The electronic edition omits printed front and back matter and editorial notes and does not encode all texts or all variants in the source volume.
notes: The selection contains a nested Moralitas in The Bludy Serk but otherwise twelve top-level poem divisions.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Verse lines, speaker labels, medicine subheadings, subscriptions, and one empty display line
editorial_practices.representation: All are represented with L elements; most verse lines have N attributes
editorial_practices.description: The XML has 893 L elements, but 25 are unnumbered labels, subscriptions, subheadings, or an empty display line, and The Bludy Serk's subscription is incorrectly numbered 121. The intellectual verse total is therefore 867 lines, not 893 or 868.

PB values follow Smith's selected-page sequence but are incomplete and anomalous: the initial Robene page 90 is absent, page 91 appears twice around lines 1-24, and Sum Practysis of Medecyne and The Thre Deid Pollis have no internal PB milestones.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/HenMino.xml.
evidence.summary: Supplies the electronic title, blanket Henryson attribution, 1993 date, OTA identifier, Smith source statement, omission declaration, c. 1480 legacy creation assertion, twelve-text boundary, headings, and encoded text.

evidence.citation: Element, numbering, and page-milestone audit of the pinned HenMino XML.
evidence.locator: All DIV1/DIV2/HEAD/LG/L/PB elements and N/NODE attributes
evidence.summary: Counts 12 DIV1, one nested DIV2 Moralitas, 13 HEAD, 106 LG, 893 L, 868 numbered L, four P, two header NOTE, and 33 PB elements. One numbered L is the Bludy Serk subscription, yielding 867 verse lines; 25 unnumbered L elements are labels, subscriptions, medicine subheads, or an empty line.

evidence.citation: Pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule state and git object inspection.
evidence.summary: Fixes the repository provenance and exact XML object used for this review.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, HenMino, Robert Henryson's The minor poems of Robert Henryson.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current public item, U-M identifier, contents, and No Copyright - United States/public-domain access designation.

evidence.citation: G. Gregory Smith, ed., The Poems of Robert Henryson, vol. III, Text vol. II, Scottish Text Society 58 (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood and Sons, 1908).
evidence.locator: title page; preface pp. vii-x; selected texts pp. 90-168
evidence.summary: Complete source-edition scan supporting the bibliographic record, source-version table, page ranges, texts, subscriptions, and collateral versions.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for ThePoemsOfRobertHenrysonVol3.
evidence.summary: Supplies exact public filenames, PDF checksum metadata, file inventory, and item identity for the cached volume III derivatives.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of Smith volume III source and textual-ascription tables and the twelve selected texts.
evidence.summary: Identifies the exact selected version, source witness, page range, line count, and printed source subscription for every HenMino text and separates unselected parallel versions.

evidence.citation: Line, heading, subscription, and page-milestone comparison between HenMino.xml and Smith volume III.
evidence.summary: Confirms Smith as the immediate source and documents the twelve selected versions, omissions, PB anomalies, title typo, display-line encoding, and the genuine 91-line Medecyne text despite Smith's 90-line table entry.

evidence.citation: G. Gregory Smith, ed., The Poems of Robert Henryson, vol. I (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood and Sons, 1914).
evidence.locator: title page; introduction; notes on shorter poems
evidence.summary: Complete critical volume supplying Smith's historical source and authorship discussions omitted from the electronic item.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for poemsofroberthen01henr.
evidence.summary: Supplies exact public filenames, file inventory, and item identity for the cached volume I derivatives.

evidence.citation: Smith, The Poems of Robert Henryson, vol. I (1914), introduction and notes on the shorter poems.
evidence.summary: Discusses source uniqueness and attribution, including the Gray sole witness, Bannatyne/Maitland conflict for The Thre Deid Pollis, the later-hand Prayer ascription, and the Bannatyne source for Medecyne.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.1.1.6, Bannatyne manuscript.
evidence.summary: Confirms shelfmark, date, George Bannatyne's compilation, codex contents and structure, physical description, current two-volume arrangement, digital-surrogate availability, and restricted physical access.

evidence.citation: NLS IIIF Presentation manifest for Bannatyne manuscript volume I, digital object 232641635.
evidence.summary: Complete 424-canvas manifest and image bundle identifying Adv.MS.1.1.6(i), exact image services, metadata, and Public Domain Mark statement.

evidence.citation: NLS IIIF Presentation manifest for Bannatyne manuscript volume II, digital object 232641636.
evidence.summary: Complete 454-canvas manifest and image bundle identifying Adv.MS.1.1.6(ii), exact image services, metadata, and Public Domain Mark statement.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Copyright guide, Statements and licences: Digitisations.
evidence.locator: Statements and licences; Copyright statuses of items in our collections; Digitisations
evidence.summary: Current guidance states that faithful digitizations have the same copyright status as the original and that NLS waives any copyright or related rights in the digitization layer through the Creative Commons Public Domain Dedication (CC0). It also distinguishes item-specific Public Domain Mark and InC-RUU statements.

evidence.citation: Direct canvas-by-canvas inspection of the NLS Bannatyne volume I and II IIIF objects against Smith and CELM locators.
evidence.summary: Verifies all ten selected passage boundaries and direct image URLs. It also corrects The Thre Deid Pollis to fols. 57v-59r/pages 174-177/canvases 192-195 because page 178 contains the next work.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.34.7.3, Gray manuscript.
evidence.summary: Confirms shelfmark, late-fifteenth-century date, composite codex, James Gray association, physical description, current Annunciation foliation, and digital-surrogate link.

evidence.citation: NLS IIIF Presentation manifest for Gray manuscript Adv.MS.34.7.3, digital object 212467260.
evidence.summary: Complete 242-canvas manifest and image bundle with exact image services and the InC-RUU rights statement.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of NLS Gray manuscript canvases 175-178.
evidence.summary: Canvas 175 begins The Annunciation, canvas 178 finishes it before the next legal entry, and the sequence reconciles current NLS foliation with Smith/CELM's historical foliation.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, First Scottish Books: Chepman and Myllar prints.
evidence.summary: Identifies the unique 1508 Edinburgh print collection, tract sequence, printers, NLS holding context, and official image viewer.

evidence.citation: Wikimedia Commons, File:Chepman and Myllar.djvu.
evidence.summary: Supplies the exact complete 220-image public DjVu derivative and its public-domain file-page assertion.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of Chepman and Myllar facsimile images 144-145.
evidence.summary: Locates The Prais of Aige lines 1-18 on image 144 and lines 19-32 on image 145; the latter then begins Devise, Prowess and eke Humility.

evidence.citation: Catalogue of English Literary Manuscripts 1450-1700, Robert Henryson author entries and introduction.
evidence.summary: Provides the current scholarly witness census and loci for Bannatyne, Gray, Maitland, Makculloch, the 1508 print, and Riddell's untraced Abbey Walk copy. Direct images supersede one internally inconsistent Pollis endpoint.

evidence.citation: Magdalene College, Pepys Library visitor information and catalogues and research tools.
evidence.summary: Confirms advance research contact, the temporary closure to general visitors through 2027, and the absence of a complete online Pepys manuscript catalog or facsimile.

evidence.citation: University of Edinburgh Archives Online, Makculloch manuscript, MS 205.
evidence.summary: Confirms shelfmark, historical designations, 1477 Leuven host text, later Scots additions, physical extent and support, reading-room access, and limited rather than complete online images.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, current General Introduction to the Complete Works of Robert Henryson.
evidence.summary: Supports the Older Scots literary context, biographical uncertainty, witness overview, and warning against treating every shorter poem as unquestionably Henryson's.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, current Introduction to the Shorter Poems.
evidence.summary: Supports poem-level source, genre, and attribution cautions, including the later-hand Prayer ascription and conflicting Pollis attributions.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, current Shorter Poems texts and notes.
evidence.summary: Supplies current readings and interpretive notes used to check the twelve poem summaries while remaining distinct from the 1993 encoding's source edition.

open_questions.question: Can a surviving OTA deposit record identify the exact production workflow and granularity of U-243-A beyond the 1993 U-M header?
next_steps: Ask the Oxford Text Archive or U-M for legacy accession documentation if production-level provenance becomes necessary.

open_questions.question: Who wrote The Thre Deid Pollis?
next_steps: Preserve both witness ascriptions and consult future attribution scholarship; do not normalize the conflict into a single certain author.
notes: The selected Bannatyne version ends quod patrik Iohnistoun; the Maitland version ends quod Mr Robert Henrysoun.

open_questions.question: How confidently should The Prais of Aige, The Abbey Walk, and Ane Prayer for the Pest be assigned to Henryson?
next_steps: Keep attribution scoped to each poem and transmission layer; review new attribution studies rather than upgrading legacy collection headings.

open_questions.question: What are the individual composition dates and places of the twelve poems?
next_steps: Retain broad late-fifteenth-century Scottish context unless poem-specific evidence emerges; do not treat the XML's collection-level c. 1480 as exact.

open_questions.question: Why does Smith's source table report 90 lines for Sum Practysis of Medecyne when Smith's printed text and the XML run through line 91?
next_steps: Check Bannatyne directly against a diplomatic transcription and any errata; retain 91 as the inspected text count in the meantime.

open_questions.question: What reuse permission, if any, will NLS grant for the Gray manuscript images marked InC-RUU?
next_steps: Seek written permission from NLS before publishing or redistributing the cached Gray images; retain the cache for private research validation only.

open_questions.question: What is the current precise NLS shelfmark for the mounted Chepman and Myllar print collection formerly designated Advocates Library 19.1.16?
next_steps: Ask NLS rare-books staff or consult the current rare-books catalog before citing a present shelfmark.

open_questions.question: Does the Abbey Walk copy reported by David Riddell in 1636 survive under an unidentified shelfmark?
next_steps: Follow future CELM revisions and search Riddell-related catalog records; do not infer readings or a stemmatic position without the copy.
